Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd try to carve out time on weekends or late evenings, but it was pretty scattered. With limited resources and just trying to get by financially, studying often took a backseat to other life stuff. I'd maybe get in about 9 hours a week, but it was never consistent blocks of time—more like grabbing an hour here and there when I could.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines more than any real schedule. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd have an assignment due and I'd cram everything in. I wanted to be more consistent, but life just kept getting in the way. The anxiety about falling behind would spike, and then I'd have these intense bursts of activity followed by nothing.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about a deadline or getting a notification that something was due. Sometimes the gamified tasks would catch my attention—those little badges and progress bars made me curious enough to log in. I wasn't one of those people who checked daily; I'd go days without logging in and then suddenly realize I needed to catch up.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The gamified elements helped a bit—I'd get drawn in by wanting to complete a challenge or see my progress update. But I never got to the point where logging in felt automatic. It always required conscious effort, and with my anxiety about the course, sometimes that effort felt overwhelming even though I knew I should do it.
Q5: The reading materials were my lifeline because that's how I learn best—I need to read and write things out. The videos were useful too; I watched a lot of them, way more than I probably needed to. I'd rewatch sections when the written stuff didn't click. The gamified tasks actually helped motivate me more than I expected—they made the boring stuff feel like less of a chore.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums and peer interaction stuff entirely. I know that sounds bad, but I just couldn't bring myself to engage with other students. The anxiety made it feel like too much exposure, like people would judge me for not being as prepared. I also skipped some of the supplementary readings when I was short on time.
Q7: If something directly connected to an assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. The gamified tasks were clever because they made even review activities feel worthwhile—like I was accomplishing something tangible. But if something seemed like extra fluff or wouldn't affect my grade, I'd skip it. I had to be strategic with my limited time and energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required stuff had this weight to it—I'd do it even when my anxiety was screaming at me to avoid everything. Optional things? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The gamified optional tasks were different though; they felt less threatening somehow, more like a game than schoolwork.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone so I could study during my commute or when I didn't have good internet. I'd write out notes by hand because that's how information sticks for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer—reviewing printed materials, making flashcards, that kind of thing. None of that would show up online.
Q10: There were whole weeks where I was actively studying from downloaded materials and handwritten notes, but my LMS activity looked dead. I'd watch a video once, take detailed notes, then study those notes for hours offline. Or I'd print out readings and annotate them on paper. My best studying happened with pen and paper, not clicking around online.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once, download it or screenshot the important parts, and then work with it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed and go. The LMS would show one login, one download, but that material might fuel a whole week of studying on my end.
Q12: My attendance was pretty rough—under 50%—and there were definitely periods where everything felt impossible. When my anxiety spiked, even opening the LMS felt like too much. Financial stress would pile up, I'd miss classes, fall behind, and then the shame of being behind made it harder to re-engage. It was this awful cycle.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS became something I'd avoid. Fatigue made it hard to concentrate on videos, and confusion about assignments would make me shut down instead of asking for help. Competing responsibilities meant I'd sometimes go a week without logging in, then scramble to catch up.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd log in and just... scroll. Click through things without really absorbing anything. My eyes would be on the screen but my mind was somewhere else, worrying about other stuff. I'd watch a video and realize at the end I hadn't retained a single thing. That happened more than I'd like to admit.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because the thought of other people reading what I wrote made me anxious. I'd avoid starting assignments until the last minute because the pressure of doing them 'right' felt paralyzing. Even when I knew something was important, the anxiety would convince me I'd do it better later. I never did it better later.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. My LMS activity probably looked like nothing, nothing, nothing, then a huge spike right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but that panic was the only thing stronger than my avoidance. The gamified elements helped a little by creating mini-deadlines that felt less scary.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it was like permission to breathe for a minute. My exam scores were actually pretty good, which surprised me and gave me confidence to keep going. But waiting for feedback was torture—my anxiety would spiral imagining the worst. I'd avoid checking grades for days sometimes, then finally look and feel relieved.
Q18: The course having 12 modules was overwhelming to look at all at once. I did better when I could focus on one thing at a time. The gamified structure helped because it broke things into smaller, achievable chunks. But the overall structure assumed a level of consistent engagement I just couldn't maintain with everything else going on in my life.
Q19: The gamified tasks genuinely helped—they made engagement feel less like a chore and more achievable. But the peer interaction requirements were hard for someone like me with anxiety. The reading-heavy approach worked well for my learning style. What made it harder was when things weren't clearly organized or when I couldn't easily find what I needed quickly.
Q20: I think some people might be searching for what they need, trying to understand the system, or just anxious and clicking around without direction. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Someone could be lost, confused, or procrastinating by reorganizing their approach instead of actually doing the work. I've definitely been there.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity was inconsistent and my attendance was terrible, but I still pulled a Distinction. I did a lot of learning offline—reading, writing notes, reviewing on my own. The system can't see the hours I spent with printed materials or the way I'd think through concepts during my commute. Performance doesn't always match platform presence.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked and for how long, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss offline studying, mental engagement, and the quality of what you're doing. They might show I watched a video, but not that I took three pages of notes. They definitely can't capture the anxiety that made logging in feel like climbing a mountain some days.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions. It's when your brain is actively working on understanding something, whether that's online or off. For me, engagement happened most when I was writing out notes or connecting ideas in my head—not necessarily when I was clicking around the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I worked to overcome my anxiety just to participate at all. It wouldn't show the offline hours with my notes, the mental energy spent worrying about the course, or how the gamified elements actually helped me feel less overwhelmed. It also can't show that despite my messy engagement patterns, I genuinely learned and cared about doing well.
